ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cuttlefish food product class; no specific dried/dehydrated cuttlefish subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried cuttlefish as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical snack or flavouring portion of dried cuttlefish is approximately 20 g, equivalent to a small handful of strips or one small whole piece.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Dried Cuttlefish (F03040028)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for dried cuttlefish (cr_id F03040028). All core macros and reported micronutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat of 0.4 g/100 g is cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 15149 (Cuttlefish, mixed species, cooked, moist heat) which reports a comparable saturated fat fraction; the HPB record does not separately itemise saturated fat so this value is adopted from USDA SR Legacy as a supporting secondary source.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dried cuttlefish contains only 2.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g with 0 g sugar and 0 g dietary fibre. As a high-protein, near-zero-carbohydrate seafood product, glycemic index is not applicable and no GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per 20 g serving is 0.0. Per the Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for dried cuttlefish, consistent with the not-applicable classification for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1744 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Dried cuttlefish is contraindicated as a regular food for hypertensive individuals due to sodium at 1744 mg/100 g, which far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold; it should be avoided or used only in very small quantities (5–10 g) as an occasional flavouring agent, and soaking in water prior to use may partially reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (2.1 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact, but high cholesterol (316 mg/100 g) and very high sodium (1744 mg/100 g) require caution
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried cuttlefish poses no glycaemic risk due to its negligible carbohydrate content and very high protein (65.3 g/100 g), but diabetic individuals with concurrent dyslipidaemia or hypertension should limit intake to small flavouring portions (5–10 g) given the high cholesterol and sodium levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol (316 mg/100 g) and very high sodium (1744 mg/100 g) present compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should strictly limit dried cuttlefish consumption due to its very high cholesterol content (316 mg/100 g) and extreme sodium load (1744 mg/100 g), both of which can adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; use only as a minor flavouring ingredient in portions of 5 g or less.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (1744 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue oedema; low saturated fat and high protein are neutral to mildly positive
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should use dried cuttlefish only in small flavouring amounts due to the high sodium content which can exacerbate tissue swelling, and should be aware that the high cholesterol content warrants additional caution for those on corticosteroid therapy with elevated cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried cuttlefish is a dehydrated seafood product made from whole or sliced cuttlefish (Sepia spp.) that has been salted and air-dried or mechanically dehydrated to remove most of its moisture content. The resulting product has a yellowish-white to brown colour, a very firm, chewy, and dense texture, and a concentrated umami-rich, savoury, and slightly briny flavour. It is widely used across Southeast Asian cuisines — particularly in Singapore, Malaysia, China, and Hong Kong — as a snack food, a flavouring ingredient in soups and braised dishes, and as a component in traditional recipes such as bak kut teh accompaniments and Teochew-style congee. Nutritionally, dried cuttlefish is exceptionally high in protein at 65.3 g per 100 g, making it one of the most protein-dense seafood products available. Its fat content is very low at 1.9 g per 100 g with only 0.4 g saturated fat, and carbohydrate content is negligible at 2.1 g per 100 g, rendering it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. However, its sodium content is extremely high at 1744 mg per 100 g and cholesterol is elevated at 316 mg per 100 g, making it clinically unsuitable as a primary protein source for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or dyslipidaemia. Iron content is notably high at 23.9 mg per 100 g and potassium is substantial at 1261 mg per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Typical serving sizes are small — 10 to 20 g — when used as a flavouring or snack component.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in dried cuttlefish?
Is dried cuttlefish high in sodium?
Can hypertensive patients eat dried cuttlefish?
How to reduce sodium in dried cuttlefish

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F03040028) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a supporting secondary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dried cuttlefish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry (F03040028) as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15149) does not provide a direct dried cuttlefish entry with full fatty acid profile. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Food Composition Database for this food item. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dried cuttlefish is a natural seafood product with no industrial trans fats). sugar_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no sugars reported in HPB source). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — seafood product contains no dietary fibre). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (2.1 g/100 g, 0 g sugar); glycemic_index set to null per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. saturated_fat_g of 0.4 g cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15149) as the HPB record does not separately itemise saturated fat. FoodOn match is closest_parent — no specific dried cuttlefish subclass exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04-16. FoodEx2 code A0CJL used for cuttlefish dried product as exact match.

Organization is 'Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore' — an approved primary regional source. URL points to the HPB food nutritional composition search tool.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 confirmed zero — seafood product), sodium_mg (1744.0), saturated_fat_g (0.4), sugar_g (0.0 confirmed zero) all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) per critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source.source_match_type is 'analogous_estimate' (a valid enum value). gi_source documents the rationale including reference to University of Sydney GI Database not listing a value for this food. Q4 passes under the not-applicable pathway.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn match_type is 'closest_parent' with honest gap note. FoodEx2 code A0CJL (Cuttlefish, dried) used as exact match. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.
